Self-driving cars are poised to be the most groundbreaking technology of the 21st century. I eagerly anticipate the day when I can hop into one of these vehicles. Imagine being able to sightsee, enjoy a martini, or work from the comfort of your car. This exciting prospect comes with numerous benefits, including the potential to reduce traffic congestion through the "platooning effect," where autonomous cars maintain optimal speeds and spacing, alleviating the frustrations caused by human drivers.

Moreover, the implementation of self-driving cars could significantly decrease law enforcement costs. With fewer traffic stops for minor infractions, taxpayers would save money. The elimination of personal car insurance is another appealing notion, as it would lessen financial burdens on drivers. Instead of circling parking lots in search of a spot, these vehicles could drop passengers off at their destinations, encouraging a healthier lifestyle by prompting individuals to walk a little more.

While there are challenges to address, highly skilled engineers are equipped to tackle the disadvantages of self-driving technology. In the case of accidents, liability would likely fall on human operators, ensuring a legal framework could adapt. Concerns about job displacement are valid; however, many new opportunities in sectors like retail, fast food, and healthcare could emerge, allowing displaced workers to find new employment or receive retraining.

Additionally, the cost-effectiveness of self-driving technology could lead to cheaper goods, as autonomous trucks would operate continuously, eliminating the need for human drivers. This transition would also benefit the environment by reducing the number of trucks on the road. Initially, these vehicles may be more accessible to the affluent, but, like all technology, prices will eventually decrease, making them available to a broader audience. 

Self-driving cars would empower individuals with disabilities, allowing them to easily attend medical appointments without relying on public transport. The advanced algorithms in these vehicles would enhance safety by obeying traffic laws and minimizing accidents, ultimately saving time and resources for our judicial system. The reduction of frivolous lawsuits would also diminish the presence of unscrupulous lawyers seeking profit.

In summary, the advantages of self-driving cars are extensive—they promise to save time and money while creating excellent job opportunities for infrastructure development. I eagerly await the day when I can join the ranks of those embracing this innovative technology.

draft1:
In today's age we are always looking for the 'next thing' in terms of technological advancement. Google is one company that continuously strives to answer that call. Recently they have been doing so by researching the technology for and creating self-driving cars. While this may sound like some futuristic fabrication from Back to the Future, the technology is actually here and being tested even as this piece is being written. 
  Self-driving cars would drastically change our mobility. Human error accounts for about 81 percent of car accidents; if everyone were to transition to self-drivings cars, human error would be eliminated and accidents would be projected to drastically decrease. Traffic patterns themselves would change. The technology in the car would be able to detect its proximity to other cars and adjust its speed and positioning accordingly. This has the potential to make roadways more efficient and commute shorter. One of the most attractive aspects of a self-driving car is the convenience that they provide. The New York Times has suggested that these cars would be a great asset to individuals with disabilities; instead of relying on public transportation they would be able to operate their car as anyone else would. These cars would also be an asset to an individual who wants to get work done in the time it takes to make their commute; they could safely work on a laptop or hold a conversation without having to worry about driving. 
  At this point you're thinking, 'this seems great, sign me up!', right? Well, you might want to re-consider. Self-driving cars sound great in theory but thinking through their practical applications one runs into several road blocks (pun intended). Consider this. A person gets into their car while intoxicated. In theory, this wouldn't be a problem because the car can simply drive itself and the individual can safely ride in the back without endangering themself or others. The car comes upon a traffic signal that is not operating correctly and there is a traffic cop directly the flow of cars. The self-driving car is not equipped to deal with human signals; it does not have human judgement. Instead of being able to over-ride the autonomic function of the car the individual is incapacitated themself and so the car speeds through the signal resulting in an accident where several people are injured. Whose fault is this? The intoxicated individual? The car? The other cars involved? The answer is not clear cut here but the result is the same as if an intoxicated individual had chosen to manually drive the car.
  Humans are not perfect. But then again, neither is technology. Both may have the ability to drive a car but is one really better than the other? My response would be no. A self-driving car is an amazing feat of technology. It is a tool that can be used for many positive purposes and has the potential to change the way in which humans travel. But self-driving cars should only be that: a tool. Humans should still retain the capacity to manually drive a car should the technology glitch and fail for both their safety and the safety of others.

	At this point you're thinking, 'this seems great, sign me up!', right? Well, you might want to re-consider. Instead of being able to over-ride the autonomic function of the car the individual is incapacitated and so the car speeds through the signal resulting in an accident where several people are injured. Prior to the adoption of mass-production, we must be careful to define the role that human's will play while inside the vehicles. However, the sooner these topics are discussed the easier it will be to resolve potential problems that arise regarding these cars in the future. Learning how to safely use this new technology will be the cornerstone upon which the technology ultimately proves to help or hinder our mobility and the transportation industry as a whole.

In today's world, we are constantly seeking the latest advancements in technology. Google is one company that consistently rises to this challenge, particularly through its research and development of self-driving cars. While this may seem like something out of a science fiction movie, the technology is real and currently undergoing tests as this essay is being composed.

Self-driving cars promise to revolutionize our transportation systems. Human error is responsible for approximately 81 percent of car accidents; thus, transitioning to self-driving vehicles could significantly reduce these incidents by removing human error from the equation. Additionally, the technology in these cars can assess their surroundings, adjusting speed and positioning to optimize traffic flow. This could lead to more efficient roadways and shorter commutes. One of the most appealing features of self-driving cars is their convenience. According to The New York Times, these vehicles could greatly benefit individuals with disabilities, allowing them to travel independently without relying on public transportation. Furthermore, commuters could utilize their travel time more effectively by working on laptops or engaging in conversations without the distraction of driving.

However, before you eagerly sign up for this new technology, it is important to consider the potential pitfalls. While self-driving cars sound remarkable in theory, their practical applications raise numerous concerns. For instance, imagine a person entering their vehicle after consuming alcohol. Theoretically, this should not pose a problem since the car can drive itself, allowing the individual to relax in the back seat. However, if the car encounters a malfunctioning traffic signal and a police officer directing traffic, it may struggle to interpret human signals. Lacking human judgment, the self-driving car could fail to respond appropriately, leading to an accident where multiple people are injured. This scenario raises difficult questions about accountability: is it the fault of the intoxicated passenger, the car, or the other drivers involved? The answer is far from straightforward, yet the outcome mirrors that of a traditional accident involving a drunk driver.

While humans are inherently imperfect, technology is not infallible either. Both have the ability to operate a vehicle, but is one truly superior to the other? I contend that neither is unequivocally better. Self-driving cars represent an extraordinary technological achievement, serving as tools that could transform our travel experience. However, these vehicles should remain just that: tools. It is crucial for humans to retain the ability to manually operate a vehicle to ensure safety in the event of technological failures or glitches.

Therefore, benefits--such as reduction of distracted driving and time in the car for other activities--have their limits, as drivers will need to be paying enough attention to take over in an emergency situation.

a second major concern: safety and privacy. The articles mentions several features that would help with safety, such as reducing human error and determining appropriate stopping distances. Therefore, while the benefits are significant, they are limited by the necessity for drivers to remain vigilant and ready to take control in emergency situations.

output_text:
Many car accidents are preventable, and a significant portion of them results from distracted driving. Self-driving cars may not be the solution we seek.

Equipped with numerous sensors and sophisticated algorithms, self-driving cars have the potential to be ideal drivers; they are more accurate, reliable, and focused than human operators. However, engineers are still conducting research on this technology, and many practical questions remain unanswered. For instance, who will take control if the technology fails? This op-ed will argue against the widespread adoption of self-driving cars.

First, like humans, technology can fail. When such failures occur, who will assume responsibility for driving? Self-driving cars are outfitted with a prominent sensor system that acts as the “eyes” and “ears” of the vehicle. In adverse conditions, a human driver relies on their senses to navigate safely. However, if the car’s sensors are compromised in extreme weather, it becomes “blind” and incapable of safe operation. Moreover, the existence of self-driving cars may discourage individuals from pursuing driver’s education. When technology fails, untrained human operators may be ill-equipped to take over, leading to potentially dire consequences.

Who should be held accountable for the outcomes of a malfunctioning self-driving car? In the event of a malfunction, passengers may struggle to regain control in time to prevent an accident. The delay between a mechanical failure and the attempt to regain control could be sufficient to result in a collision. In legal proceedings, determining responsibility in such cases can be exceedingly complex. We desire safety, and that requires dependable technology.

Proponents of self-driving cars contend that they will reduce driving costs and alleviate societal burdens. However, the price of self-driving technology is often much higher than anticipated. The research funding that companies are currently investing in testing will ultimately be passed on to consumers. Additionally, the sensors used in self-driving cars come with substantial maintenance and replacement costs. As a result, these vehicles remain a luxury for many. If self-driving cars are not widely accessible, the purported benefits cannot be realized. Furthermore, the transportation industry may face rising unemployment rates as machines replace human labor, and the gasoline industry could experience significant downturns as self-driving cars are likely to be electric.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars present intriguing possibilities, the challenges and risks associated with their adoption far outweigh the potential benefits.

Self-driving cars have beneficial and disadvantageous effects on society. Although the benefits of self-driving cars may seem promising, the adverse effects might deter one from investing in them as the future of automobile technology. First, let’s review the positive side of self-driving cars. 

Self-driving cars would allow parents to feel safer about their high-school children “driving” to school with a reduced risk of speeding or an accident. If their child is distracted by a text, it wouldn’t matter because he/she wouldn’t need to pay attention to the road. If everyone had self-driving cars, there would be much fewer accidents, and speeding would be a thing of the past, allowing police officers more time and manpower to devote to other crimes. These self-driving cars would also benefit the sick and the elderly who would otherwise need to depend on others to get around. 

From an industrial growth prospective, the move toward self-driving cars would promote job growth for minds with a knack for technological advancement and machine learning. A new industry would be created. This can translate to the public transportation sector; self-driving buses and trains may soon follow. 

However, although there are many positives of self-driving cars, as with any technological advancements, there are also risks. Those with self-driving cars may need to worry about keeping their data secure from hackers who may want to know a person’s most frequently visited locations. This data could be used in targeted advertisements, or for more malicious purposes such as ascertaining the best place for a potential robbery or when the home would be empty. In adverse weather or road conditions that would provide the self-driving car with less-clear visual data, there could be a greater risk for an accident. If the occupants of the car do not pay careful attention, they may end up in a car accident. Even more alarming, if a self-driving car is on a slippery road and spins out of control, does it jerk out of the way to save a child pedestrian or the fit male pedestrian? The car wouldn’t know how to make these “gut-feeling” decisions. Additionally, if an accident does happen, where does the blame lie? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter. If the driver was not technically in control of the vehicle, they may not be responsible either. All that is left is the manufacturing company, however that doesn’t seem appropriate either. New legislation would need to be implemented to account for these possible scenarios.

Whereas some industries may see job growth, public transportation personnel would have a job reduction, as would taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially harming export sales from countries that would need it. If these self-driving cars are mostly electric, many gas stations may go out of business. 

Thus, although self-driving cars may provide some benefits to society, the overall risks and potential harm is far greater. People would lose the ability to do things for themselves, others may exploit holes in the technology’s security to harm others, and industries may collapse due to the sudden shift in technology that would ultimately change how the world functions.

Self-driving cars present both promising advantages and concerning drawbacks for society. While the potential benefits of autonomous vehicles are enticing, the negative implications may lead one to hesitate before endorsing them as the future of transportation. To begin, let's explore the positive aspects of self-driving cars.

One of the key benefits of self-driving vehicles is the enhanced safety they offer, particularly for parents concerned about their teenage children commuting to school. With the reduced risk of speeding and accidents, parents can feel more at ease knowing their children are "driving" without the need for constant attention to the road. If everyone utilized self-driving cars, we could expect a significant decrease in accidents, freeing up law enforcement to focus on other pressing issues. Furthermore, self-driving technology would greatly assist the elderly and disabled, providing them with newfound independence in transportation without relying on others.

From an economic standpoint, the transition to self-driving cars could spur job creation in fields related to technology and machine learning. This shift may also extend to the public transportation sector, potentially leading to the introduction of self-driving buses and trains, thereby transforming the way we think about mass transit.

Nevertheless, alongside these advantages, there are significant risks associated with this technological advancement. One major concern is the potential for data breaches. Self-driving cars collect and store data about users' travel habits, which could be exploited by hackers for targeted advertising or even criminal activities, like planning burglaries based on when a home is unoccupied. Additionally, self-driving cars may struggle in adverse weather conditions, leading to a heightened risk of accidents. If passengers become complacent and fail to monitor their surroundings, the consequences could be dire. Furthermore, in critical situations, such as deciding between swerving to avoid a child or an adult pedestrian, these vehicles lack the intuition that human drivers possess. 

The legal implications of accidents involving self-driving cars also pose a challenge. If an incident occurs, determining fault becomes complicated. Drivers might argue they were not in control, yet holding manufacturers accountable raises its own set of issues. Thus, new legislation would be necessary to address these dilemmas.

While some sectors may witness job growth, others, such as public transportation and taxi services, could face significant layoffs. The oil and gas industries might also suffer, particularly if self-driving cars predominantly operate on electric power, leading to a decline in demand for gasoline and potentially resulting in the closure of gas stations.

In conclusion, although self-driving cars offer certain benefits, the overarching risks and potential societal harm are considerable. The shift toward autonomous vehicles could diminish personal agency, expose vulnerabilities in technology, and disrupt entire industries, fundamentally altering how we navigate the world.

output_text:
As technology rapidly advances, innovators are increasingly exploring the potential of self-driving cars. What once seemed like a child's imaginative vision of a futuristic society is now becoming a reality, and the implications of these vehicles on our lives could be substantial. Self-driving cars evoke images of stress-free travel, as they promise to alleviate the burdens of driving by allowing technology to take over. By removing the human element from the equation, these vehicles aim to reduce accidents, relying on programming that adheres to traffic laws and avoids collisions. However, this optimistic view overlooks the significant challenges that accompany self-driving technology. 

The adoption of self-driving cars presents risks that may outweigh their benefits. Proponents argue that these vehicles will not suffer from distracted driving, as they follow precise algorithms while on the road. While it is true that human drivers face numerous distractions, they possess instinctual reactions that machines cannot replicate. The critical concern lies in the decision-making capabilities of self-driving cars. Although their algorithms may appear superior to human judgment, the complexity of real-world situations often requires nuanced decision-making. For instance, consider a scenario where a self-driving car is cruising past a grocery store, and suddenly a shopping cart rolls into its path. In such a split-second moment, the car’s programming might dictate swerving to avoid the cart. However, if a pedestrian is crossing nearby, how will the car make the split-second decision to prioritize the safety of the human over the cart? Unlike machines, humans can instinctively assess and react to such situations, often making choices that prioritize life and safety.

This example illustrates just one of the countless scenarios that highlight the limitations of self-driving cars in replicating human instinct and judgment. With numerous uncertainties and inherent risks associated with autonomous vehicles, it is wise to approach the implementation of self-driving cars with caution. The potential negative outcomes appear to overshadow the benefits they may offer to society, making it prudent to reconsider the push for their widespread adoption.

As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with the implementation of that technology. This is also true with autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With the testing of self-driving cars currently happening in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether or not autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of the public health and wellness debate in the media. Many cars already come equipped with some of the basic technology - autonomous breaking, self-parking, or sensors that detect close objects, so we may be closer to the future than we think.

There are several pros for the use of self-driving cars, and one of the biggest is safety. While a small percentage of car accidents could be caused by mechanical failures, the majority are caused by a distracted driver. A computer driver would take the human element factor out of the equation and remove most of the danger just by being a computer. Computers make for the ideal driver as they will not get tired, lose their eyesight, get distracted, imbibe in drugs or alcohol, etc. Computers also use specific algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other collected information that help to dramatically decrease the chance of an accident. 

In addition to safety, there are many other positive side-effects to autonomous driving. Self-driving cars could help with traffic optimization, as the cars could communicate with each other to help identify traffic problems and their specific algorithms and sensors could help to reduce commute times while also allowing more cars on the road with less traffic. People with disabilities would have more options at their disposal. Police could focus less time on traffic stops and more time on other crimes. People would have more time to do other thigns with less parking and DMV headaches for drivers. The benefits are endless. 

With all the good things that come with technology, there are bound to be some bad things, as well. One of the biggest downfalls anticipated with self-driving cars would be their affect on the economy. Not only is the cost of implementing the new technology more expensive than most Americans can afford, it could also eliminate a lot of jobs in transportation. It is also expected that since most self-driving cars are powered by computers, that they would be electric cars, further putting a strain on the gas industry. Reliance on technology could make a driver's license, insurance, the DMV, and other driving related vendors obsolete. The benefits of self-driving cars would also depend upon more people opting into the option. 

Other downfalls to autonomous cars would be the flaws with technology itself. A computer cannot detect a road block or read human hand signals, so road construction would be an obstacle. The sensors are located on the outside of the vehicle, so severe weather can damage them. Computers attract hackers and software requires user information, so there is always a chance for breach of security. Another risk would be the technology failure where a person must take over the controls - what if it is someone that has never driven their own car before? What if the GPS goes down? How will a person be able to get to where they need to go?

While a completely autonomous driving society is probably a ways off, society certainly seems to be moving towards it. I do believe that self-driving cars would be a benefit in many ways - safety, inclusion, environment, and time/traffic - but I think we have some questions that need to be answered before we get there. While there may be jobs that are lost in transportation, there will be jobs that are created with the technology and manufacturing of these vehicles. The inclusion of people with disabilities, the elderly, and those that do not have a license is a powerful tool for those that were previously reliant on others. In my opinion, self-driving cars will get over the hump and be a part of the near future, at least in a small realm.

output_text:
The emergence of any new technology invariably brings with it a mix of advantages and disadvantages. This is particularly true for autonomous, or self-driving, cars. As cities across the United States test these vehicles, the debate regarding their safety compared to human-driven cars has gained significant traction in public discourse. Many vehicles already incorporate basic autonomous features—such as automatic braking, self-parking, and object detection sensors—suggesting that the future of driving may be closer than we realize.

One of the most compelling arguments in favor of self-driving cars is their potential to enhance safety. While mechanical failures account for a small percentage of accidents, the majority are attributed to human error, particularly distracted driving. Autonomous vehicles eliminate the human factor, thereby reducing the risk associated with tiredness, distraction, and impaired judgment caused by drugs or alcohol. Moreover, these vehicles utilize advanced algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other critical data, significantly lowering the likelihood of collisions.

Beyond safety, the benefits of self-driving cars extend to various other aspects of society. For example, autonomous vehicles could improve traffic flow by communicating with one another to identify and resolve traffic issues, ultimately leading to decreased commute times and increased road capacity. Additionally, individuals with disabilities would gain greater independence, while law enforcement could redirect their focus from routine traffic stops to addressing more serious crimes. The potential for reduced parking challenges and fewer trips to the DMV would also free up time for drivers, resulting in a myriad of positive outcomes.

However, along with these advantages come notable concerns. One major drawback is the economic impact of self-driving cars. The high costs associated with implementing this new technology may be prohibitive for many consumers, and widespread adoption could lead to job losses in the transportation sector. Furthermore, as these vehicles are likely to be electric, there may be repercussions for the fossil fuel industry. The shift toward autonomous driving could render traditional driving-related services, such as obtaining a driver’s license or insurance, obsolete, complicating the transition for many.

There are also inherent risks associated with technology itself. Self-driving cars may struggle with roadblocks or human signals during construction, and adverse weather can impair sensor functionality. Additionally, the reliance on software invites the possibility of hacking, raising security concerns. Another critical issue is the potential for technology failure, leaving an untrained driver in a precarious situation. For instance, if a driver has never operated a vehicle before, how will they respond if they suddenly need to take control?

While a fully autonomous driving society may still be years away, it is evident that we are progressing toward that future. I believe that self-driving cars will bring numerous benefits—enhancing safety, promoting inclusivity, supporting environmental goals, and improving time efficiency—though several questions remain unanswered. Although there is a risk of job losses in transportation, new opportunities will arise in technology development and manufacturing. The empowerment of individuals with disabilities, the elderly, and those without licenses represents a significant leap forward for a population that has historically relied on others for mobility. In my view, self-driving cars will eventually overcome their current challenges and become a valuable part of our near future, at least in specific contexts.

I believe that even with self-driving cars, human drivers should still be held accountable for accidents, except in cases of clear manufacturing errors, as the responsibility to operate the vehicle safely ultimately lies with the human.

Self-driving cars represent a significant technological advancement, and their presence on our roads is becoming increasingly common. While some critics express concerns about potential increases in fatalities, costs, and legal complexities associated with this innovation, the benefits of self-driving cars appear to substantially outweigh these drawbacks. 

One of the primary arguments against self-driving cars stems from fears of heightened accident risk. Critics contend that if no human is at the wheel, the likelihood of errors by the computer system increases, potentially leading to accidents or fatalities. While this perspective highlights valid concerns—particularly in light of reported incidents involving self-driving vehicles—it is crucial to consider the broader context. Research indicates that 81 percent of car crashes result from human error. By transitioning to self-driving technology, we can eliminate the majority of these accidents. Moreover, any accidents involving autonomous vehicles are expected to be significantly fewer than those caused by human drivers.

Another common argument against self-driving cars is their initial implementation cost. The expenses related to advanced software, sensors, and extensive research and development can be substantial. However, this viewpoint often overlooks the potential for considerable savings in insurance premiums. The U.S. Department of Transportation assigns a value of $9.2 million to a human life, and insurance companies utilize similar figures when assessing the costs of fatal accidents. Should a self-driving car be involved in an accident—though such occurrences are anticipated to be rare—the financial responsibility would shift away from individuals, leading to lower insurance and healthcare costs. Additionally, self-driving cars can provide users with more free time by handling driving tasks, effectively translating saved time into monetary benefits.

Finally, concerns about the lack of legal precedents for accidents involving self-driving cars are often raised. Critics argue that ambiguity surrounding liability and damage compensation could lead to complex legal disputes. While these concerns are legitimate, stifling technological advancement due to potential legal challenges would hinder progress and prevent the development of solutions for contemporary and future issues. If self-driving cars can pave the way for a safer, more efficient, and cost-effective future, it is imperative for society to support the successful integration of this technology.

output_text:
**Embracing Self-Driving Cars and Moving Away from the Gasoline Industry**

Self-driving cars represent a crucial advancement in transportation, promising to significantly reduce accidents and diminish the negative environmental impacts associated with the gasoline industry. Automated vehicles, controlled by sophisticated computer systems, will eliminate the distraction that often leads to human errors—responsible for 94% of traffic accidents. With the average human attention span being a mere eight seconds, self-driving cars can react more swiftly and consistently than their human counterparts, addressing the dangers posed by distracted driving.

One of the primary advantages of self-driving cars is their potential to mitigate accidents, particularly those caused by distraction. Drivers often engage in risky behaviors such as texting, eating, or adjusting their music, all of which compromise their ability to operate a vehicle safely. By removing human error from the equation, we can significantly reduce accidents caused by not just distractions, but also speeding, reckless driving, and other hazardous behaviors. 

Moreover, self-driving vehicles are likely to be electric, offering substantial environmental benefits. Electric cars are more efficient and produce less pollution compared to traditional gasoline-powered vehicles. They operate quietly and smoothly, making for a more pleasant driving experience while being cost-effective—operating at about one-third to one-quarter the cost of gasoline. Additionally, electric cars require less maintenance, as they do not need oil changes, and their lack of tailpipes contributes to improved air quality.

The gasoline industry has long been a significant contributor to air pollution. The harmful emissions from gas-powered vehicles can lead to severe health issues, including respiratory problems and long-term exposure risks like cancer. With fossil fuels becoming increasingly scarce, transitioning to electric vehicles is not just beneficial but necessary for a sustainable future.

It is essential to recognize that the shift away from gasoline-powered cars will not lead to job losses without alternatives. Mechanics can be retrained to service electric vehicles, adapting to the changing landscape of the automotive industry. Just as society adapted when transitioning from horse-drawn carriages to cars, we can navigate this change without sacrificing employment. Public transportation options, such as buses and taxis, will continue to provide jobs for those who do not own cars.

In conclusion, I firmly advocate for the adoption of self-driving and electric vehicles as a means to enhance road safety and protect our environment. By embracing this technology, we can move toward a cleaner, safer, and more efficient future, leaving behind the outdated and polluting gasoline industry.

The evidence lies in the fact that we can not seem to stay focused on the road for the duration of a car ride without checking our phones for insignificant messages and calls. These seemingly innocent phone checks while driving end up claiming the lives of thousands per year despite laws against texting or calling while on the road. Self-driving cars have become a contentious issue, sparking discussions about their various advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, they promise greater efficiency in transportation, enhanced mobility for everyone, potentially safer roads, and an overall boost in quality of life. Conversely, they pose threats to jobs in the transportation sector, present risks in the event of a malfunction, struggle to interpret human intentions, and depend on sometimes unreliable GPS systems. With such significant pros and cons, one might wonder if the benefits truly outweigh the risks. While society may not necessarily need self-driving cars, the trajectory of recent technological advancements suggests they could be a logical next step. Ultimately, self-driving cars are advantageous for society because their potential benefits surpass the manageable challenges they introduce.

The advantages of self-driving cars are compelling enough to overshadow the criticisms against them. It is evident that humans are increasingly reliant on technology, often unable to stay focused on the road during drives without checking their phones for trivial notifications. These distractions result in thousands of fatalities each year, despite strict laws prohibiting phone use while driving. For instance, Maryland, known for its stringent regulations on cell phone use, still ranks among the states with the highest rates of poor driving and fatal accidents. While people may continue to ignore these laws, a self-driving car will not be distracted. Unlike humans, the computer systems in self-driving vehicles are designed to maintain focus. Critics may argue that computers can malfunction, but it is essential to recognize that traditional cars often experience breakdowns as well. Numerous stories exist of drivers facing sudden brake failures that lead to accidents. Moreover, regular maintenance and software updates mandated by car manufacturers can effectively minimize the likelihood of malfunctions in self-driving cars. Most concerns regarding these vehicles are resolvable issues that do not warrant a permanent aversion to their development. For example, the criticism that self-driving cars cannot interpret human traffic signals can be addressed by programming them to recognize situations, like a traffic light outage with a human directing traffic. The vehicle could analyze the situation or consult the human passengers for guidance. Although this presents challenges, there are enough brilliant minds in the world capable of addressing similar issues. From environmental benefits to reduced traffic congestion and improved mobility for the elderly and disabled, the advantages of self-driving cars are numerous and too valuable for society to overlook.

In conclusion, self-driving cars offer more advantages than disadvantages, advocating for their integration into the future of technology. By increasing public awareness of how to effectively manage and mitigate the potential downsides, more individuals may embrace this innovation, leading to a safer and more convenient lifestyle. The choice is clear: the technology is available, and the only real question is whether society is ready to move into the future.

Picture this: you can relax in an air-conditioned vehicle, take a nap for half an hour, and arrive at your destination without the stress of navigating traffic or searching for parking.

Like any innovation, self-driving cars present both advantages and challenges. On one hand, travelers can enjoy protection from adverse weather conditions, but on the other hand, there is a risk that the car's sensors could malfunction, leading to a loss of control.

Additionally, self-driving cars may eliminate the need for insurance coverage, as the vehicle's computer would be responsible for decision-making. However, this raises concerns about accountability; in the event of an accident, determining liability could become complicated.

This technology has the potential to revolutionize healthcare delivery, enhance traffic management, and provide more convenient parking solutions. However, critics argue that self-driving cars are still in the developmental stage, where there is little room for error, particularly given the potential for loss of human life and financial repercussions. The unpredictable nature of the driving environment poses significant challenges. For instance, a drunk driver could unexpectedly alter the road dynamics, or a child might unexpectedly run into the street.

After weighing the pros and cons, I believe that self-driving cars are essential for the future. A compelling argument in favor of this technology is its successful implementation in airplanes and automatic people movers at airports.

Major technology companies are fully backing this innovation, which could significantly improve the lives of individuals with disabilities. output_text:
Society is continually evolving and adapting, particularly in relation to technology. Products that are now available to the public would have been unimaginable just a few years ago. One futuristic concept currently undergoing testing is self-driving cars. This technology holds significant appeal for various reasons: increased accessibility, a perceived reduction in accidents, and potential traffic efficiency, among others. Conversely, there are substantial drawbacks to integrating self-driving cars into society: high costs, potential software malfunctions, a decline in driving skills, and the threat of job loss for many individuals involved in driving-related professions. Despite the potential benefits of self-driving cars, the disadvantages ultimately outweigh the advantages for society.

According to the article, a primary motivation for the push toward self-driving cars is the expectation that they will dramatically reduce road accidents; however, numerous factors must align perfectly for this to occur. Passengers must still possess a basic understanding of how to operate the vehicle’s mechanics to intervene in emergencies. With the possibility of human error, there is already a fundamental flaw in the notion of being accident-free. Additionally, adverse weather conditions can impair the vehicle’s laser sensors, necessitating that passengers remain vigilant in case of malfunctions, which again opens the door to accidents caused by human oversight. Furthermore, no car can be programmed to account for every local driving law, potential road hazard, or construction detour. GPS systems, which are not always reliable, can be corrected by manual driving; if a self-driving car lacks accurate information, it could lead to accidents. There is no guarantee that self-driving cars will significantly decrease the occurrence of accidents. Moreover, the presence of these vehicles may complicate legal proceedings when accidents do occur. Questions about liability arise: Is the car at fault? The manufacturer? The passenger?

Self-driving cars pose more risks than benefits concerning society and the public at large. For one, the majority of people may not be able to afford them. Current estimates place the cost of engineering and technology at around $100,000. To enjoy even the minor benefits of self-driving cars, they would need to be widely adopted, which seems impractical. Moreover, widespread implementation would render numerous essential jobs obsolete. Auto manufacturers, driver education institutions, personal injury lawyers, and gasoline industries would all face significant challenges in finding work. Consequently, many people could be laid off, negatively impacting the unemployment rate and the economy as a whole.

While technology offers great advantages in modern society, it should only be applied where truly necessary. Would you want to live in a world where people have lost the ability to operate a vehicle? A society that becomes so dependent on technology that a glitch or recall could leave them stranded without transportation? An innovation that threatens the livelihoods of countless working Americans? Why confront these dilemmas when manual driving could alleviate numerous economic, ethical, and safety concerns?

A world with self-driving cars may not be too far off, as companies like Google have been testing vehicles capable of driving themselves in certain states. One of such controversial topics currently being discussed widely is self-driving cars. Academic institutions and industry have started testing the practical use of the self-driving technology that requires no drivers to drive, but as the self-driving technology can potentially risk human's life by causing more car accidents that human drivers could prevent, people are very concerned and skeptical about its use. These people, because they cannot drive a car, need to use public transportation or assistance when they go shopping, travel, and/or commute. This can be very hard, especially in a location where public transportation is not developed and/or in busy cities where other people are always in hurry.

When there is no evidence that self-driving cars cause more accidents, it is reasonable and more important to promote self-driving cars which can create a better transportation opportunity for people having a hard time driving and using other means of transportation.

It also minimizes risks associated with the self-driving technologies in the future as we collect driving data, which would decrease the number of car accidents and therefore offer a better experience for people.

output_text:
Technological advancements have undeniably enhanced convenience and efficiency in various aspects of our lives, from social networking and shopping to personalized streaming services. However, the debate over whether these technologies ultimately improve or detract from society persists. One of the most talked-about innovations is self-driving technology, which is being developed to create safer vehicles through advanced algorithms. Despite the potential for increased road safety, many individuals express skepticism about self-driving cars, primarily due to concerns over their reliability and the risk of accidents. While I acknowledge these risks, I firmly believe that self-driving technology offers significant opportunities for individuals facing mobility challenges and can improve continuously through data analysis.

Self-driving cars hold great promise not just for the general population but also for those with disabilities, pregnant women, and others who struggle with driving. Many of these individuals rely on public transportation or assistance for their travel needs, which can be particularly challenging in areas with limited public transit options or in bustling urban environments. By providing a personalized and accessible transportation solution, self-driving cars can greatly enhance the mobility of those who are unable to drive. While there are inherent risks associated with this technology, such as difficulties navigating adverse weather conditions or complex traffic scenarios, it is essential to recognize that human drivers also pose significant risks due to distractions, intoxication, or fatigue. Since driving will always carry some level of risk, it makes sense to explore self-driving cars as a means to improve transportation for those who struggle with conventional options.

Moreover, the aforementioned risks can be mitigated through data collection and analysis. One of the key benefits of self-driving cars is their ability to capture detailed data when accidents occur, allowing for thorough investigations into the causes. This data can then be used to refine algorithms and prevent similar incidents in the future. In contrast, identifying the precise causes of accidents involving human drivers is often challenging, and even when causes are determined, immediate improvements are rare. Although educational initiatives and policy reforms aimed at reducing accidents exist, they typically require significant time to manifest tangible results.

In conclusion, I believe that the introduction of self-driving cars can create new opportunities for those facing difficulties with traditional transportation methods. Additionally, as we gather and analyze data from these vehicles, we can mitigate the associated risks, ultimately reducing the incidence of accidents and enhancing the overall experience for all road users.

In our rapidly developing society, new technology has an impact on so many new industries, especially in transportation. Currently, many cars have features such as motion sensors and autonomous braking. As these features become more prevalent, there is a question of whether self-driving cars will eventually take over the roads. Self-driving cars are determined to be safer than human driven cars due to the removal of human error. They are also proven to be more efficient because the computer has the driving responsibility, and more accessible to those who are not able to drive cars themselves. Unfortunately, there are some disadvantages to self-driving cars. Computers are unable to respond to human signals and unpredictable situations, which may be a serious safety concern. The use of self-driving cars may also have a negative effect on the economy because it eliminates many jobs such as taxi drivers, personal injury lawyers, and drivers ed instructors. However, the advantages in safety, efficiency, and accessibility of self-driving cars outweigh these possible risks and demonstrate that self-driving cars would be a very beneficial addition to our society.

Safety is the most notable advantage of self-driving cars. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors and instead use precise algorithms to drive, this greatly reduces the risk of accidents. Self-driving cars, however, have some disadvantages that human driven cars do not, such as risk of malfunction in certain weather conditions and inability to follow human traffic signals. There is also a question of what the driver may have to do if the technology malfunctions. However, these are all situations that occur infrequently. The situations in which self-driving cars would save lives occur much more frequently, showing that the safety advantages outweigh the possible risks.

Self-driving cars also have many advantages when it comes to efficiency in cost and time. With fewer accidents on the road, money will be saved for insurance and healthcare. Drivers are also able to use time on the road to complete other tasks since they don’t have to focus on the road. In large groups, self-driving cars participate in platooning. The cars are able to communicate with each other, which helps to reduce traffic and hazardous situations on the road. This saves time, gasoline, and also allows cars to travel at higher speeds, further saving driving time. Overall, the efficiency of self-driving cars reduces traffic and makes the road safer.
	
Finally, the accessibility of self-driving cars gives access to transportation to those who cannot drive cars themselves, such as disabled and elderly individuals. Disabled individuals often rely on others for transportation or have to use public transportation, greatly inhibiting their mobility. Access to self-driving cars would allow these individuals to travel in a more convenient way, granting them more freedom. This is true for elderly individuals as well. People often worry about allowing their elderly parents drive at a certain age, concerned about their safety but not wanting to take away their independence. With self-driving cars, this would not be an issue.
	
Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology increases safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

While these concerns are certainly notable, the safety advantages of self-driving cars offset the possible risks. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors, the chance of an accident is greatly reduced. Computers are able to make calculations in order to stop a safe distance away from other objects, decreasing the probability of a crash. Self-driving cars will save many lives and should be considered in spite of the possible concerns. 
	As technology continues to advance, there are many new possibilities of improvement in transportation. Self-driving cars may sound like an invention from the future, but they will likely be on the roads sooner than expected. Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology improves safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

In our rapidly advancing society, technology significantly influences various industries, particularly transportation. Many modern vehicles now feature advanced technologies such as motion sensors and autonomous braking systems. As these innovations become increasingly common, the question arises: will self-driving cars eventually dominate our roads? Self-driving cars are deemed safer than those operated by humans due to the elimination of human error. They also enhance efficiency, as computer systems manage driving tasks, and they improve accessibility for individuals unable to drive. However, self-driving cars come with certain drawbacks. For instance, computers struggle to interpret human signals and respond to unpredictable scenarios, raising safety concerns. Additionally, the widespread adoption of self-driving technology could negatively impact the economy by displacing jobs such as taxi drivers, personal injury lawyers, and driving instructors. Despite these potential challenges, the benefits of safety, efficiency, and accessibility presented by self-driving cars far outweigh the risks, making them a promising addition to our society.

Safety stands out as the most significant advantage of self-driving vehicles. Research indicates that 81 percent of car accidents result from human error. Unsafe driving can stem from distractions, driving under the influence, or speeding. In contrast, computers are immune to these factors, utilizing precise algorithms to navigate safely, thus reducing the likelihood of accidents. Nonetheless, self-driving cars do face unique challenges that human drivers do not, such as potential malfunctions in adverse weather and difficulties in interpreting human traffic signals. There is also the concern of how a driver should react during a technology failure. However, these issues are infrequent compared to the numerous scenarios where self-driving cars could prevent fatalities, demonstrating that the safety benefits significantly outweigh the potential downsides.

Moreover, self-driving cars offer substantial advantages in terms of cost and time efficiency. With a decrease in accidents, savings on insurance and healthcare costs are likely. Additionally, passengers can utilize their travel time for other activities since they are not focused on driving. In coordinated groups, self-driving cars can engage in platooning, communicating with one another to mitigate traffic and hazardous conditions. This capability not only saves time and fuel but also allows for higher travel speeds, further enhancing efficiency. Overall, the introduction of self-driving cars can alleviate traffic congestion and promote safer roads.

Lastly, the accessibility of self-driving cars provides vital transportation options for individuals unable to drive, such as the elderly and those with disabilities. Many disabled individuals depend on others for transportation or public transit, which can severely limit their mobility. The availability of self-driving cars would grant these individuals greater independence and convenience in their travels. Similarly, families often hesitate to allow elderly parents to drive due to safety concerns while also wanting to respect their autonomy. Self-driving vehicles would alleviate this dilemma, allowing seniors to maintain their independence without compromising safety.

While self-driving technology is still evolving, it promises to enhance safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If successfully developed, these vehicles could positively transform the transportation sector and society as a whole.

draft1:
I marvel at the ingenuity of our scientists and engineers in bringing us so close to the possibility of self-driven vehicles. 

Furthermore, I am a free spirit who cherishes her independence, including the freedom to go where she pleases when she pleases, without having to rely on other people, their schedules, their preferences and especially their notions about what I, as a woman of a certain age, should or should not be doing. All this makes me deeply empathetic toward seniors and the disabled who may not be able to drive, and for their sakes, I can deeply appreciate the value of a self-driven vehicle.

Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could mean the end of our reliance on fossil fuels,  something deeply to be desired by all of us who are worried about the future of our polluted planet.

And yet, I find that something deeper, perhaps even less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driven, at least for the present.

We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. In cases where computers are absolutely better than us at certain things, e.g. the analysis of large quantities of crucial health-related data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But 
I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them, in areas where they are not particularly better than we are. Driving is a case in point. You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 

And then, there is the questions of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation?  You may argue that we are everyday and everywhere rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, nor can the cars of anyone else, and you're all,  every single one of you, headed for disaster with no recourse, where at least if you were at the wheel you could have attempted some solution. It is this removal of human agency that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which 
can be the source of virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. I do not wish to be thus reduced.

At home, this pattern of behavior continues, sadly. We are losing the ability to be with each other, whether that means having rich and complex and spontaneous interactions or simply occupying the same space, something that is not as mindless and mundane and trivial as it might appear, as psychologists and neuroscientists are beginning to understand. Self-driven cars, by eliminating the need for public transportation will only deepen this social atomization.
          The advocates of autonomous vehicles claim that eliminating public transportation cuts infrastructure costs, and they might even say, it would make for greener environs. But it is not clear whether the rise in the number of autonomous vehicles will not require comparable infrastructure, as the majority must drive such vehicles if they are to be a feasible option in the first place.

We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. Nor can the cars of anyone else. And you're all headed for disaster with no recourse. Rendered utterly passive where once you might have attempted some solution, no matter its efficacy. 
	It is this removal of human agency, individual and collective,  that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but rather thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which can breed virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing.

Today's society is seeing the start of a new era in technology, where artificial intelligence is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. This includes the gradual integration of self-driving cars, or cars that rely on computer programming to give it directions.

This can be a great improvement to driving safety, as this is potentially a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible -- they can get distracted, feel sleepy, become under the influence -- and drastically increase their chance of accidents. Computers, however, do not succumb to such mistakes.

Additionally, they are able to communicate with other computers easily, so if there were multiple self-driving cars on the road, they can all sense each other within seconds and know how to navigate from there.

However, computers are not 100% fool-proof. They are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on more people to "opt-in" and drive their own self-driving car, otherwise, accidents will still happen.

But the thing is, dear reader, that all these cons can be remedied with time. We are currently seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation, but the idea of relying on new technology to propel us forward in society is a long-standing tradition. This is a natural process.

The more we work on this technology, the safer it will become. We will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. We will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily, or at all. And the more we improve upon it, the more widespread the technology will be, which will cause its pricepoint to decrease and in turn make self-driving cars much more accessible. 

There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology and then it decides to stop working, or it turns against us?" To that, dear reader, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we can use self-driving cars does not mean we will stop learning how to drive.

But, dear reader, I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever.

In today's society, we are seeing the start of a new era in technology where artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. Examples of this can be found in our homes, social media, everyday retail, and other facets of our lives. 
	Many dystopian books and films would like you to believe that the steady integration of AI is the start of our societal downfall, but this is simply not the case--take self-driving cars for example. For those of you who are unfamiliar, self-driving cars are vehicles that rely on AI to give itself directions and to literally drive itself without any human interaction. 
	In general, this is a great improvement to driving safety as it can provide a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible--they get distracted, they get sleepy, and they can choose to drive while under the influence--which can drastically increases the chance of accidents. Computers, however, are literally unable to succumb to such mistakes and are therefore safer. 
	Additionally, computers are able to communicate with other computers easily, unlike humans who have no form of communication with any of the other drivers in close proximity. This advantage allows computers in self-driving cars to sense each other within seconds and negotiate the road together. 
	However, many people think that if we were to rely on these AI-driven cars, it would become a gradual demise for us. Self-driving cars are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on people en masse to "opt-in" and buy their own self-driving cars, otherwise a single self-driving car cannot communicate with a vehicle which cannot "talk back."
	But what these naysayers are forgetting is that all of these downfalls can be remedied with time--we are only seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation. It is a natural process to introduce new technology into society and wait for newer models to come out until it is perfected. By dismissing the new technology in its early stages without giving it the chance to improve with time would be like telling a child to stop practicing at something because they were awful in the beginning. 
	Here is a simple example of how we can slowly rely on technology to assist us: traffic lights. Way before we relied on traffic lights to help guide us through intersections, we had traffic guards to help us. But over time, we allowed ourselves to accept traffic lights, instead of a human, to help us through. And of course, the traffic light wasn't perfect or even similar to the version we have today! It also had a simpler code and fewer systems to help us, but we let it stay up and now we have near-perfect traffic light controls and coordination systems. 
	The point is, the more we work on this technology, the safer it will become, but only if we give it a chance to grow and perfect itself. This is not to say that the negative points aren't valid or accurate observations on the current self-driving cars we have today. But we tend to forget that this is not an indication of what it could be. Of what it could provide for us. And how helpful and much safer it could actually be for us in the long-term. 
        Manufacturers, scientists, and computer scientists alike will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. Will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily. And with further improvement, the more common the technology will become and cause its pricepoint to decrease, which in turn will make self-driving cars much more accessible and allow a wider population to purchase their own.
	There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology, and then it decides to just stop working or turn against us?" To that, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot feature in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we give cars the knowledge of the road does not mean that all of a sudden it is going to drive off without us.
	But I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever, and if you are one of the few who miss such a time, then maybe self-driving cars were never meant for you to begin with.

In today's world, we are entering a new technological era where artificial intelligence is becoming increasingly prevalent, leading to greater reliance on advanced systems. A significant development in this realm is the gradual adoption of self-driving cars, which utilize sophisticated algorithms to navigate roads.

The potential benefits of self-driving cars are considerable, particularly in enhancing road safety. Human drivers are prone to errors—distracted driving, fatigue, and impaired judgment can significantly elevate the risk of accidents. Conversely, computers operate without these vulnerabilities, potentially reducing the number of accidents on our roads.

Moreover, self-driving cars have the capability to communicate with one another seamlessly. When numerous autonomous vehicles are on the road, they can detect each other's presence almost instantaneously, allowing for coordinated navigation and improved traffic flow.

Nonetheless, it is important to acknowledge that these systems are not infallible. Self-driving cars can be susceptible to hacking, can be misled, and their effectiveness hinges on widespread adoption; without enough vehicles on the road, the risk of accidents remains.

However, these challenges can be addressed over time. We are only at the beginning of this automation revolution, but history shows that society has consistently embraced new technologies to enhance our lives. This evolution is expected and natural.

As we continue to refine this technology, its safety will improve. Future software updates will enhance security against hacking, while advancements in artificial intelligence will boost their spatial awareness and adaptability. As these improvements occur, the technology will become more prevalent, ultimately lowering costs and making self-driving cars accessible to a broader audience.

Concerns about a dystopian future often surface, with questions about over-reliance on technology. "What if the system fails or turns against us?" I would argue that such fears belong to the realm of science fiction. The introduction of calculators did not eliminate the need for learning mathematics, just as autopilot systems in aircraft do not negate the necessity for pilots to learn traditional flying skills. Similarly, the availability of self-driving cars will not diminish the importance of knowing how to drive.

However, I will say this with conviction: the manual transmission deserves to fade into obscurity.

draft1:
Society remains divided on the issue of self-driving cars. Some people think they’re great: they should reduce accidents, free up drivers’ time, and make obsolete the need for detailed knowledge of driving, thus letting students learn other subjects in school and allowing people who could never use cars alone in the past (like the disabled and elderly) more freedom. Meanwhile, others focus on the problems. Computers would not make decisions and adapt in the same manner as attentive human drivers, provide more security risks, and present a large potential for vehicle failure. I side with this latter group, and my main reasoning is that there are more weaknesses and things that can go wrong with a computer than with a human.
I’ve got a little story on computers who’ve been trained to act like they can drive better than humans can. In April my parents and I were in Florida visiting my grandparents. We had a rental car and my father was driving on the highway. A car in front of us was going far below the speed limit, so my father punched the accelerator in order to pass it. The rental car’s computer determined that he must have meant to hit the brakes and hit the accelerator by mistake. So, the car suddenly locked up on the busy highway and stopped, causing all passengers to be thrown forward and the car behind us to nearly rear-end us. There was no human error involved here. No distracted driving. Just a system that has been trusted and deployed into millions of cars on the road, activating without the driver’s knowledge or consent. This “feature” is just a taste of what interacting with full-blown self-driving cars would be like.
Autonomous vehicles pose even more risks when we move beyond considering bad software that only influences how the car interacts with the driver and begin to think about security and how the computer handles driving in varying environmental conditions and around other drivers. According to the Guardian, the self-driving cars’ computers would be a target for hackers. There are so many situations where a self-driving car could be led to cause accidents, especially if the human behind the wheel has never learned to drive or is too distracted or too trusting in the vehicle’s technology to intervene. A simple idea is that a hacker can encourage the cars to engage in a platooning pattern (where the cars line up closely behind each other like train cars), at which point the cars would be like a line of little ducklings ready to do the bidding of Mother Duck, leading them off the road or suddenly slamming on the brakes when the cars are very close and travelling at high speeds. 
Many proponents of self-driving cars act on the presumption that vehicle computers have an understanding of the environment around them, and in fact would be even more aware than a distracted human would be. Sure, if they’re equipped with the right sensors they’ll know the speeds of nearby cars, they’ll acknowledge the possibility that a child or deer could run onto the road at any moment, and they’ll probably monitor weather conditions. But in terms of anticipating the behavior of other drivers, they fall short. When I drive, I can look at other vehicles and anticipate their behavior. This driver is turned around looking at their kid in the backseat. This person’s truck has tree branches in the back that aren’t tied down. This person’s car has been rear-ended several times and the bumper is being held on with duct tape. Each of these pieces of information influences what I expect the driver to act like. A computer isn’t going to look at its surroundings and judge everything like this. As such, a computer will not be as well-informed or adaptive as humans, because we will never be able to anticipate all the different types of drivers that the computer would need to be ready to judge. 
Overall, there is no debate that a self-driving car would behave differently from a human-driven one. Much of this discrepancy is due to the fact that computers are endowed with the ability to be truly conscious and can be easily mislead or misinformed.

Consider a personal experience that illustrates the shortcomings of computer-controlled driving. In April, while visiting my grandparents in Florida, my parents and I rented a car. My father was driving on the highway when a vehicle ahead was traveling well below the speed limit. In an attempt to pass, he pressed the accelerator. However, the car’s computer mistakenly interpreted this as an intention to brake, causing the vehicle to abruptly stop in the middle of a busy highway. This sudden halt threw passengers forward and nearly resulted in a collision with the car behind us. This incident highlights the dangers of relying on technology that can malfunction without any human error or distraction involved. It serves as a glimpse into the unpredictable nature of fully autonomous vehicles.

The risks associated with autonomous vehicles extend beyond software malfunctions that affect driver interaction; they also encompass security vulnerabilities and the vehicle's ability to navigate various environmental conditions alongside other drivers. According to The Guardian, the computers in self-driving cars could become prime targets for hackers. There are numerous scenarios in which a hacked self-driving car might cause accidents, particularly if the human passenger has never learned to drive or becomes overly reliant on the vehicle's technology. For instance, a hacker could manipulate the cars to form a platooning pattern, causing them to behave like a line of ducklings, easily led off course or forced to brake suddenly at high speeds, which could lead to catastrophic consequences.

Many advocates for self-driving cars assume that vehicle computers will possess a superior understanding of their surroundings compared to distracted human drivers. While it is true that, if equipped with the right sensors, these cars can detect the speeds of nearby vehicles and identify potential hazards like children or deer on the road, they still fall short in predicting the behavior of other drivers. As a human driver, I can assess the actions of other vehicles: a driver turning around to tend to a child, or a truck with unsecured branches in the back. Each of these observations informs my expectations about how other drivers will behave. In contrast, a computer cannot interpret its environment in this nuanced way. Consequently, it lacks the adaptability and intuition that human drivers possess, making it less effective in responding to the myriad of unpredictable driving behaviors.

In conclusion, it is undeniable that self-driving cars will operate differently from those driven by humans. This difference stems from the inherent limitations of computers, which lack the consciousness and judgment necessary to navigate complex and dynamic driving situations. As we consider the future of transportation, it is crucial to weigh these risks against the potential benefits of autonomous vehicles.

output_text:
The self-driving car represents a transformative leap in technology, but its integration into everyday life in America raises significant concerns. The potential benefits of self-driving cars are substantial: they promise safer driving by reducing accidents, eliminating distractions that human drivers face, detecting traffic issues more effectively, and removing the need for a designated driver, ultimately saving time and money. Conversely, the drawbacks are equally alarming. Self-driving cars are highly dependent on weather conditions, struggle to interpret human traffic signals, may malfunction and endanger occupants, rely on technologies that are not yet fully developed, and threaten jobs in the transportation sector.

At this juncture, I believe it is premature to introduce self-driving cars into mainstream society. We need further advancements in technology to ensure a seamless transition when these vehicles are deployed, minimizing negative impacts on external stakeholders. While self-driving cars could provide significant societal benefits in the future, the present is not the right time for their widespread adoption.

If self-driving cars were to be introduced today, numerous challenges would arise. A significant portion of the population would find them unaffordable, with costs exceeding $100,000 and the need for specialized training to operate them safely. Moreover, the success of self-driving cars hinges on widespread acceptance, which is unlikely if only the affluent can access them. Additionally, security concerns loom large, as hackers might exploit vulnerabilities in the technology, potentially compromising personal data.

Even if self-driving cars were made affordable for the general public, the economic implications would be severe. Many jobs in the transportation industry would be at risk, the gasoline sector could suffer if vehicles transition to electric power, driving education programs might become obsolete, and personal injury attorneys could see a decline in business due to fewer accidents. These factors could significantly disrupt the lives of many individuals and harm the economy. Therefore, I conclude that 1) the general public's adoption of self-driving cars is not feasible at this time, and 2) in the unlikely event of universal adoption, the economic consequences could be damaging.
